title: How to Use Intuition When Facing Major Life Changes
meta_description: Learn to trust your inner guidance when life feels uncertain. This article shows how intuition becomes your most reliable tool during major transitions.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Facing Major Life Changes
og_description: Learn to trust your inner guidance when life feels uncertain. This article shows how intuition becomes your most reliable tool during major transitions.
body:
You're Standing at the Edge of Something Big

Maybe you're staring at a job offer that would uproot your whole life. Maybe you're looking at a relationship that's clearly over but you can't bring yourself to leave. Maybe you just got a diagnosis, or a divorce filing, or a phone call that changed everything in a single sentence.

Major life changes don't knock politely. They kick the door in. And when you're someone who feels things deeply - someone whose nervous system picks up on every subtle frequency in the room - these moments can feel like standing in a hurricane with no shelter.

Here's what nobody tells you about big transitions: your intuition isn't just a nice-to-have when things get hard. It's the only thing that will actually get you through. Not your logic. Not your five-year plan. Not what your mother thinks you should do. Your intuition.

But here's the problem. Most of us have been trained to distrust that inner voice. We've been told it's "too emotional" or "not rational" or "just anxiety." So when the ground starts shaking beneath our feet, we reach for spreadsheets and pro-con lists and advice from people who've never walked our path. And we wonder why we still feel lost.

I'm Kalesh. I teach people how to work with their psychic sensitivity - not as some woo-woo parlor trick, but as a practical tool for living a real life. And I'm going to be straight with you: using your intuition during major life changes isn't about getting a magic answer that makes everything easy. It's about learning to trust yourself when everything around you is telling you not to.

Your intuition isn't a crystal ball that shows you the future. It's a compass that points you toward your next right step - even when you can't see the whole path yet.

Why Your Brain Will Lie to You Right Now

Let's start with what's actually happening in your nervous system when you face a big change. Because if you think you can just "think your way through" a major life transition, you're setting yourself up for a world of pain.

Your brain has one job: keep you alive. And from your brain's perspective, change is a threat. Even good change - a promotion, a move to a city you love, ending a relationship that's been draining you for years - your brain registers it as danger. Why? Because the unknown could kill you. That's how ancient your operating system is.

So when you sit down to make a rational decision about your life, your brain is already flooded with stress hormones. Your prefrontal cortex - the part that does logical thinking - is partially offline. And you're sitting there wondering why you can't "just figure it out." Right?!

This is where intuition comes in. Not as a replacement for thinking, but as a way to access information that your conscious mind can't reach when it's under siege. Your intuition speaks through your body, through your gut, through those inexplicable feelings that don't seem to have a logical basis. And those feelings? They're actually picking up on data that your rational mind is too overwhelmed to process.

The First Thing You Need to Do (And It's Not What You Think)

When a major life change hits, your first instinct is probably to figure it out. To make a plan. To get control. Stop. Don't do that yet.

The first thing you need to do is get quiet. Not in a "meditate for an hour" way - though if you can do that, great. I mean physically, literally, stop moving and stop trying to solve the problem for five minutes.

Here's why. Your intuition is quiet. It's a whisper, not a shout. And right now, your mind is screaming at you with fear-based thoughts, worst-case scenarios, and urgent demands for action. You can't hear a whisper in a room full of sirens.

So the first practice is simple: sit down somewhere comfortable. Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths - not deep breathing exercises, just normal breaths that you pay attention to. Then ask yourself one question: "What do I actually feel right now, in my body?"

Not what do you think. Not what should you feel. What do you actually feel? A tight chest? A knot in your stomach? A buzz of energy in your hands? Just notice it. Don't try to change it. Don't try to interpret it. Just let it be there.

This is the foundation of intuitive work. You can't trust your intuition if you can't even feel your body. And most of us have been dissociating from our bodies for so long that we don't even know what "feeling" means anymore.

How to Tell the Difference Between Intuition and Fear

This is the question I get more than any other: "How do I know if it's my intuition or just my anxiety?" And honestly, it's a fair question. Because both of them can feel intense. Both of them can feel certain. Both of them can keep you up at night.

Here's the difference, and it's simpler than you think. Fear feels tight. Intuition feels open.

Fear says "you have to do this or else." Intuition says "this is the direction that feels aligned, and you can trust it." Fear is urgent, demanding, and comes with a sense of panic. Intuition is calm, patient, and comes with a sense of knowing - even if that knowing is uncomfortable.

Let me give you an example. Say you're considering leaving your job. Fear-based thoughts sound like: "If I leave, I'll never find another job. I'll run out of money. Everyone will think I'm a failure. I can't afford to make this mistake." Notice the tightness in your body when you read that. That's fear.

Intuitive guidance sounds different. It might say: "This job is draining you. You know you're meant for something else. You don't have to have it all figured out, but you do need to take the first step." Notice how that feels in your body. There's no urgency. There's no panic. There's just a quiet, steady truth that you've known all along.

Does that land? Good. Because the distinction is everything.

A Practical Tool for Making Decisions Under Pressure

Okay, so you've gotten quiet. You've started to feel your body again. You can sort of tell the difference between fear and intuition. Now what? You still have to make a decision, and the clock is ticking.

Here's a technique I teach my students for exactly this situation. I call it the "Three Doors" method. It works like this:

When you're facing a choice, don't try to think your way through it. Instead, close your eyes and imagine each option as a door in front of you. Just visualize it - doesn't have to be elaborate, just a simple mental image of three doors, each labeled with a different choice.

Now, one at a time, imagine yourself walking up to each door. Don't open it yet. Just stand in front of it. Notice what happens in your body. Does your chest relax? Does your stomach clench? Do you feel a pull toward the door, or a resistance? Do you feel curious, or do you feel dread?

This isn't about which door "feels good." Sometimes the right door feels scary. But it doesn't feel tight. It might feel like a combination of fear and excitement - what some people call "the thrill of the unknown." The wrong door feels heavy, constricted, like you're shrinking just by standing near it.

Once you've stood in front of each door, notice which one you keep coming back to. Which one draws your attention even when you try to look away? That's your intuition speaking. It doesn't always shout. Sometimes it just points, gently, and waits for you to pay attention.

This technique works because it bypasses your analytical mind and goes straight to your body's wisdom. Your body knows things your brain hasn't figured out yet. And in times of major change, your body is often the only thing you can trust.

If you want to go deeper with this kind of practice, there's a book that changed how I work with intuition and decision-making. It's called The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. Don't let the title fool you - it's not about being scared. It's about learning to trust the signals your body sends you, especially when your mind is trying to override them. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When You Can't Feel Anything at All

Some of you reading this are thinking, "That's great, Kalesh, but I can't feel anything. I'm completely numb. I've been through so much that my intuition feels dead."

I hear you. And I need you to know: your intuition isn't dead. It's just buried under layers of survival mechanisms. When you've been through trauma - big T or small t - your nervous system learns to shut down to protect you. It's like a circuit breaker that trips when the current gets too high. The sensitivity is still there. It's just gone underground.

The way back isn't through forcing yourself to "feel more." It's through gentle, consistent practice. Start with something small. Not the life-changing decision you're facing. Something tiny. Like what to eat for breakfast. Or which route to take on your walk. Or what color socks to wear.

Practice checking in with your body about these small choices. Does this feel like a "yes" or a "no" in your body? Don't worry if you're not sure at first. Just practice asking the question. Over time, the signal gets stronger. Your intuition is like a muscle that's been atrophied. You have to start with light weights before you can lift the heavy stuff.

And be patient with yourself. If you've been numb for years, it might take months to start feeling again. That's okay. Your body is doing exactly what it needs to do to keep you safe. You're not broken. You're just in recovery.

The Myth of the "Right" Decision

Here's something nobody tells you about using intuition during major life changes: there's no such thing as the "right" decision. I know, I know. That sounds like a cop-out. But hear me out.

When you're facing a big choice, you're probably looking for the option that will guarantee you safety, happiness, and no regrets. That option doesn't exist. Every choice has trade-offs. Every path has losses. The question isn't "Which choice is correct?" The question is "Which choice is mine?"

Your intuition doesn't show you the future. It shows you what's aligned with your soul in this moment. And that alignment might look different from what you expected. It might not make logical sense. It might disappoint people you love. It might scare the hell out of you.

But here's the thing I've learned from working with hundreds of sensitive people: when you make a choice from intuition, even if it's hard, even if it doesn't work out the way you hoped, you don't regret it. Because you know you chose from your truth. The regret comes from choices made from fear - from ignoring that inner voice and doing what you thought you "should" do.

There's a book that captures this idea perfectly: The Untethered Soul by Michael A. Singer. It's about learning to trust the deeper intelligence that's always guiding you, even when your mind is screaming objections. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Handle the Resistance You'll Get From Other People

Let's talk about something that comes up for almost everyone when they start following their intuition during a major life change: other people's opinions.

You're going to tell someone about your decision - the one that feels deeply right in your gut - and they're going to look at you like you've lost your mind. They're going to list all the logical reasons why you're wrong. They're going to bring up your past mistakes. They're going to tell you you're being "impulsive" or "irrational" or "too emotional."

And here's the hard truth: they're not wrong from their perspective. They're looking at your life through their lens, which is calibrated for safety and predictability. They can't see what you see. They can't feel what you feel. And they're probably scared for you, which comes out as criticism.

So what do you do? You don't try to convince them. You don't defend your decision. You don't explain your intuition to people who aren't ready to hear it. You say something like: "I appreciate your concern. I've thought about this a lot, and this is the right choice for me right now." Then you change the subject.

Your intuition doesn't need validation from anyone else. It doesn't need a committee. It just needs you to trust it. And the more you trust it, the stronger it gets. The stronger it gets, the easier it is to trust. It's a feedback loop that builds on itself.

The Role of Surrender in Major Transitions

This might be the hardest part of using intuition during big changes: knowing when to stop trying and just let things unfold.

There's a difference between surrendering and giving up. Giving up is when you stop caring and let life happen to you. Surrendering is when you stop forcing and let life work through you. It's an active, conscious choice to release your grip on the outcome and trust that you'll be guided to where you need to be.

Your intuition will often tell you when it's time to surrender. You'll feel a sense of "I've done everything I can do here." You'll feel a release in your body - that tightness you've been carrying starts to soften. You'll feel a quiet knowing that the next step will reveal itself when it's time.

This doesn't mean you become passive. It means you stop white-knuckling your way through life. You stop trying to control every variable. You stop treating your life like a problem to be solved and start treating it like a mystery to be lived.

For anyone who struggles with the surrender piece - and most of us do - I recommend The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle. It's the classic on learning to be present with what is, rather than fighting reality. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When Your Intuition Leads You Somewhere Unexpected

Sometimes you follow your intuition and it takes you somewhere you never expected. Maybe you leave a stable career to start something uncertain. Maybe you end a relationship that looked good on paper. Maybe you move to a place you've never even visited. And then, when you get there, it's harder than you thought.

Does that mean your intuition was wrong? No. It means you misunderstood what intuition is. Intuition isn't a guarantee of ease. It's a guarantee of alignment. The path that's right for you might be rocky. It might be lonely. It might require more from you than you thought you had to give.

But here's what I've seen over and over: when people follow their intuition through a major change, even when it's hard, they grow in ways they couldn't have imagined. They discover strengths they didn't know they had. They meet people and opportunities that weren't on their radar. They look back and realize that the "wrong" turns were actually the most important parts of the journey.

There's no way to avoid the hard parts of life. But there is a way to move through them with grace, with trust, with the deep knowing that you're exactly where you need to be. And that way is through your intuition.

If you want to go even deeper into trusting your inner guidance during uncertain times, check out Letting Go by David R. Hawkins. It's a practical guide to releasing the fear and resistance that block your intuitive voice. See on Amazon (paid link)

A Final Word for the Sensitive Ones

You've been told your whole life that you feel too much. That you're too sensitive. That you need to toughen up. That your emotions are a liability. I'm here to tell you the opposite is true.

Your sensitivity isn't a weakness. It's the source of your intuition. It's what allows you to pick up on the subtle energies that other people miss. It's what gives you access to a deeper wisdom than logic alone can reach. And in times of major life change, that sensitivity is your greatest asset.

The world needs people who can feel deeply and still choose to trust themselves. People who can stand at the edge of the unknown and say "I don't know where this is going, but I know I'm supposed to go." People who can feel the fear and do it anyway, not because they're brave, but because they trust the voice that's guiding them.

That's you. That's who you are. And the more you practice using your intuition - even when it's messy, even when you're not sure, even when everyone around you thinks you're making a mistake - the more natural it becomes. Until one day, you realize you haven't made a decision from fear in years. You've been living from your truth all along.
